sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 67-year-old male, presents with a 4-day history of right shoulder pain, which he attributes to a recent incident of being tackled while playing tackle football.
He reports pain on the back side of his right shoulder, around the acromioclavicular joint, and difficulty lifting objects due to pain.
He denies any numbness or tingling in his fingers, and his hand strength is preserved.
He has been taking Tylenol for pain but finds it only minimally effective.
He denies any fever, chills, or neck problems, and has no history of similar injuries to the shoulder.
He has a history of an appendectomy 3-4 years ago.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of an appendectomy 3-4 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient denies any other joint problems or orthopedic injuries, and has no other medical problems.
Current medications: The patient is currently taking Tylenol for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure is 122/76 mmHg, heart rate is 82 beats per minute, and oxygen saturation is 95% on room air.

Physical exam: The patient has pain to palpation of the posterior shoulder at the acromioclavicular joint, decreased abduction and adduction to 90 degrees, pain with supination and pronation of the right arm, and a palpable radial artery pulse.
Test Results: The patient's right shoulder x-ray shows an acute acromioclavicular joint fracture.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an acute acromioclavicular joint fracture of the right shoulder.
Problem list: Acromioclavicular joint fracture; suspected future risk of osteoporosis due to age.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam 15mg once daily for pain management.
I ordered a CT scan of the right shoulder to further evaluate the joint.
I referred the patient to physical therapy to strengthen the shoulder and avoid future surgery.
I instructed the patient to avoid heavy lifting and to use a sling for support.
Follow-up: The patient will return to the office for follow-up in 1-2 weeks to assess the healing of the fracture and to discuss further management.
